parents.father_research_notes: Dr. William Hooker (H00529, 1766-?), physician + trader at West Hampton MA; born Northampton MA Nov 26, 1766; son of Rev John and Sarah Worthington Hooker of Northampton. Per EH 1909 #529: 'William Hooker (John* John* John,3 Samuel,2 Thomas1)...m. 1798 Anna Thompson, daughter of Hugh Thompson of East Windsor CT. She died at West Hampton MA. He married No. 2, Widow of Ensign Hartland.'
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Thompson Hooker not yet in tree.

career.location: West Hampton, Norwich, and Westfield Massachusetts
career.notes: Merchant at West Hampton MA, then Norwich MA. In 1819 removed family to Westfield MA, where they remained.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved family from Norwich Massachusetts to Westfield, 1819
narrative_blocks.body: Merchant at West Hampton MA before relocating to Norwich MA where his two eldest children Lucy Ashmun and William Throop were born. In 1819 he moved the family to Westfield MA, where son Edward was born 1822 and where William and his wife Octavia would both be buried at Pine Hill Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Lucy Ashmun died at Rushville Illinois, 1839
narrative_blocks.body: His eldest child Lucy Ashmun died age 26 at Rushville IL April 17, 1839 — only seventeen months after her Massachusetts marriage to Dr Joseph Washburn Clark and five weeks after the birth of their infant son who himself died in May. The Illinois frontier had taken his daughter, son-in-law's first family, and grandson all within a year.

parents.father_research_notes: Dr. William Hooker (H00529, 1766-?), physician + trader at West Hampton MA; born Northampton MA Nov 26, 1766; son of Rev John and Sarah Worthington Hooker of Northampton. Per EH 1909 #529: 'William Hooker (John* John* John,3 Samuel,2 Thomas1)...m. 1798 Anna Thompson, daughter of Hugh Thompson of East Windsor CT. She died at West Hampton MA. He married No. 2, Widow of Ensign Hartland.'
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Thompson Hooker not yet in tree.

career.location: West Hampton, Norwich, and Westfield Massachusetts
career.notes: Merchant at West Hampton MA, then Norwich MA. In 1819 removed family to Westfield MA, where they remained.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved family from Norwich Massachusetts to Westfield, 1819
narrative_blocks.body: Merchant at West Hampton MA before relocating to Norwich MA where his two eldest children Lucy Ashmun and William Throop were born. In 1819 he moved the family to Westfield MA, where son Edward was born 1822 and where William and his wife Octavia would both be buried at Pine Hill Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Lucy Ashmun died at Rushville Illinois, 1839
narrative_blocks.body: His eldest child Lucy Ashmun died age 26 at Rushville IL April 17, 1839 — only seventeen months after her Massachusetts marriage to Dr Joseph Washburn Clark and five weeks after the birth of their infant son who himself died in May. The Illinois frontier had taken his daughter, son-in-law's first family, and grandson all within a year.

parents.mother_research_notes: Octavia Throop Hale (1754-1839) of Bozrah CT, died Westhampton MA aged 85. Not yet built (deferred).

cross_connections.display_label: Uncle executed as Continental Army spy, September 1776

narrative_blocks.header: Niece of Nathan Hale the patriot martyr
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Rev Enoch Hale of Westhampton MA was elder brother of Capt Nathan Hale — the Continental Army officer hanged as spy by the British at New York September 22, 1776. Nathan died nearly twelve years before her birth; the family memory of the executed uncle shaped her own brother's name (Nathan Hale Sr 1784-1863) and reached into her children's generation through her son's cousin marriage to her sister Sybella Hale Hall's daughter Sarah.

narrative_blocks.header: Died of dementia paralytica at Northampton, 1867
narrative_blocks.body: Died December 21, 1867 at Northampton MA age 79 — eighteen months after her husband — with cause given on her death record as 'Dementile Paralysis,' the period name for dementia paralytica, the late-stage neurological syndrome of progressive cognitive and motor decline.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at Rushville Illinois eighteen months after marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Married Dr Joseph Washburn Clark of Rushville IL on her twenty-fifth birthday September 28, 1837 and moved with him to the Illinois frontier. Eighteen months later — five weeks after the April 12 birth of their only child William Hooker Clark — she died at Rushville on April 17, 1839. The infant followed her on May 6, 1839.

death.notes: Likely died San Francisco area given final burial relocation.

burial.plot_notes: Originally interred at Lone Mountain Cemetery San Francisco; removed to Cypress Lawn Memorial Park Colma when San Francisco relocated in-city cemeteries to San Mateo County.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost first family Illinois, finally buried California
narrative_blocks.body: Lost his young wife Lucy Ashmun Hooker in April 1839 at Rushville IL and their infant son five weeks later in May. Survived them by 39 years; eventually died 1878 with California burial — interred at San Francisco's Lone Mountain Cemetery, then later removed to Cypress Lawn Memorial Park Colma when San Francisco relocated its in-city cemeteries to San Mateo County.

death.notes: Died at age 24 days, three weeks after his mother's death.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother died five days after his birth, 1839
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker Clark was born April 12, 1839 at Rushville Illinois — but his mother Lucy died five days later on April 17, leaving him in the care of his father Dr. Joseph Washburn Clark. He died May 6, 1839, twenty-four days old, never knowing his mother.

death.notes: Exact death date not in Sam-supplied sources.

career.organization: Farmers and Mechanics Bank of Hartford CT; then Continental Bank of New York City
career.location: Hartford CT, then New York City
career.notes: Cashier of the Farmers and Mechanics Bank of Hartford CT; later cashier of the Continental Bank of New York City.

narrative_blocks.header: Bank cashier in Hartford and New York City
narrative_blocks.body: Cashier of the Farmers and Mechanics Bank of Hartford CT, then cashier of the Continental Bank of New York City. Three of his six children — Henry Collins, William Augustus, and Caroline Christine — were born at Hartford while he held the Farmers and Mechanics post.

narrative_blocks.header: New York mining engineer; firm bore Hooker name
narrative_blocks.body: William Augustus built a career as a mining engineer in New York City, where his firm Hooker & Lawrence gave the family surname a commercial identity far from its New England roots. All three of his children were born in New York City between 1880 and 1885.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #4358: 'He is a mining engineer of the firm of Hooker & Lawrence, New York City.'

birth.notes: FindAGrave gives Westfield (family moved 1819 per EH H01410); EH 1909 has 'West hampton' likely a typo or family-origin reference. Using Westfield as more consistent with timeline.

death.cause: Mitral regurgitation of the heart (per death record)

career.organization: Phoenix Bank Hartford CT; Great Barrington bank; Westfield Bank
career.notes: Started as clerk in Phoenix Bank Hartford CT 1836 age 15; remained connected with that bank until 1847. Cashier of bank at Great Barrington MA 1847. Cashier of Westfield Bank, Westfield MA from 1851.

cross_connections.display_label: Mother of his wife Sarah Hall; his aunt through the Hale family
His uncle through the Hale family; married two Hooker women

narrative_blocks.header: Married his first cousin Sarah Hall, 1844
narrative_blocks.body: Married Sarah Hall (1819-1898) of Greenfield MA on October 10, 1844. Sarah's mother Sibylla Hale Hall was his own mother Octavia's sister — both daughters of Rev Enoch Hale of Westhampton — making the bride and groom first cousins and the marriage a second Hale-Hooker knot in the same generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Bank clerk at fifteen, Westfield Bank cashier
narrative_blocks.body: Became a clerk in the Phoenix Bank at Hartford CT in 1836 at age fifteen — staying eleven years before moving to Great Barrington MA as bank cashier in 1847. From 1851 he was cashier of the Westfield Bank in Westfield MA, where he remained until his death June 13, 1896 age 75.

death.notes: FindAGrave records May 23, 1900 Wellesley MA age 80. EH 1909 had given Oct 12, 1898 Cambridgeport MA — discrepancy; using FindAGrave as more reliable for vital records.

birth.notes: EH gives 'born at Hartford CT' (father Henry was cashier of Phoenix Bank Hartford 1836-1847); FindAGrave inconsistently gives Westfield. EH Hartford preferred — father was working there.

marriages.notes: EH gives Sep 10, 1879 marriage; FamilySearch records Sep 4 1879 Westfield MA — minor discrepancy.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost two infant sons in their first months
narrative_blocks.body: Of her three children with John Cotton Eastman of New York, the first two — Paul (1880) and Henry Hooker (1881-1882) — both died as infants. Only daughter Elizabeth Reed, born 1886, survived to adulthood. Lucy and John lived at New Haven CT before retiring to Concord NH, where both died in 1914 and lie together at Woodlawn Cemetery in the Bronx.

birth.notes: EH gives July 3, 1847 Hartford CT (father Henry cashier of Phoenix Bank Hartford at this date); FindAGrave inconsistently shows July 8, 1847 Greenfield MA. EH preferred.

education.notes: Williston Seminary at Easthampton MA prepared him for Williams College.

career.notes: Three years in the Paris office of Bolles Brothers investment bankers after Williams 1871 graduation.
Treasurer of the Jessup & Lafflin Paper Company of Westfield MA.
Nearly fifty-five years with Kidder, Peabody & Company Boston banking firm — per his December 8, 1932 New York Times obituary. Close friend of literary critic Gamaliel Bradford.

narrative_blocks.header: Fifty-five years with Boston's Kidder-Peabody
narrative_blocks.body: After Williams College 1871 and three years in the Paris office of Bolles Brothers (1872-1875), he spent nearly fifty-five years with Kidder, Peabody & Company — the Boston banking firm whose name would later become synonymous with twentieth-century American investment banking. His December 1932 New York Times obituary called him a 'close friend of the late Gamaliel Bradford.'

narrative_blocks.header: Williston Seminary then Williams College, 1871
narrative_blocks.body: Educated at Westfield High School, then Williston Seminary at Easthampton MA, then Williams College in the Berkshires graduating 1871. Also treasurer of the Jessup & Lafflin Paper Company of Westfield alongside his banking work — the kind of manufacturing-finance combination characteristic of late 19th-c New England merchant capital.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost Ellen and two of three children young
narrative_blocks.body: Married Ellen Jane Goodwin June 20, 1878 Pittsfield MA. She died July 31, 1901 Wellesley MA of nephritis age 44. Their daughter Sybil Hale died 1906 age 26; daughter Sarah Hale had died 1885 age 2 of uremic poisoning.

death.notes: NOT TO BE CONFUSED with the unrelated 19th-c Commander Edward Hooker, US Navy.

career.notes: Banker in New York City until 1859.

narrative_blocks.header: New York City banker until 1859
narrative_blocks.body: Born Westfield MA the year after the family's 1819 move from Norwich MA, youngest of the four William Hooker Jr / Octavia Hale Hooker children. Married Georgeanna C. Loring December 22, 1852 and was a banker in New York City until 1859 — joining his elder brother William Throop in the city's banking world before leaving Wall Street near the panic year.

burial.plot_notes: Buried at his wife Georgiana's family cemetery — she was born Cincinnati OH 1827.

career.notes: Practiced medicine at Westhampton MA per EH 1909 #529. Established the Hooker physician tradition in Westhampton continued by son Dr. Anson Hooker (H01414, 1799-1869) and through several Westhampton descendants.
Per EH 1909 #529: 'He was a physician and also a trader at West Hampton, Mass.' — supplementing physician income with a trading operation, common pattern for rural New England doctors of the period.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to ninety-four as Westhampton physician
narrative_blocks.body: Born Northampton MA 1766; died Westhampton MA February 27, 1861 at age ninety-four — extraordinary longevity spanning the early Republic through the Civil War's outbreak. Practiced medicine in Westhampton MA and also operated as a trader; both occupations supported a large family of seven children with first wife Anna Thompson before her 1810 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost wife at forty-two, remarried after two years
narrative_blocks.body: First wife Anna Thompson died Westhampton April 2, 1810 at age forty-two — leaving him with seven children ranging from twenty-four-year-old Henry down to eight-year-old Edward. William remarried 1812 to Rhoda Phelps, widow of Ensign Hartland of Hartland Connecticut. No documented children from the second marriage; Rhoda predeceased William by fourteen years.

parents.father_name: Hugh Thompson of East Windsor CT (per EH 1909 #529)
parents.father_research_notes: Per Edward Hooker 1909 #529: 'm. 1798 [sic — likely 1788], Anna Thompson, daughter of Hugh Thompson of East Windsor, Conn.' EH date '1798' may be transcription error; her 1786 first child suggests marriage 1785-86. Hugh Thompson East Windsor specific parentage not yet built.

narrative_blocks.header: Died age forty-two leaving seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Died Westhampton MA April 2, 1810 at age forty-two — leaving seven children ranging from twenty-four-year-old Henry down to eight-year-old Edward. Her son middle-named for her father Hugh Thompson (Hugh Thompson Hooker, born 1789) carries the East Windsor Connecticut Thompson family name forward.

ministry_positions.notes: Ordained Dec 5, 1753; settled there 24 years until death by smallpox

primary_residence.notes: Minister here 24 years 1753-1777. Yale 1751, ordained Dec 5, 1753.

narrative_blocks.header: Took Jonathan Edwards' Northampton pulpit, died of smallpox
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained in 1753, he became pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts — the church where Jonathan Edwards had preached until his dismissal in 1750. He held that pulpit for nearly 25 years, dying of smallpox on February 6, 1777, in his 48th year. His was the most prominent pastoral appointment held by any Hooker descendant of his generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's father, Colonel Worthington, named Worthington Parish
narrative_blocks.body: He married Sarah Worthington, daughter of Colonel Worthington of Springfield, Massachusetts — the man who gave his name to Worthington Parish in Berlin, Connecticut, the parish where the Hart and Hooker families had long been prominent. The marriage connected the Hooker line to one of western Massachusetts's most distinguished families.

narrative_blocks.header: Smallpox took the Northampton pastor at forty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: He served as pastor of the Northampton MA Congregational Church from 1753 until smallpox killed him in February 1777 at forty-seven. His daughter Sarah Hooker married the Northampton lawyer Caleb Strong nine months later — passing the Hooker name into the family of a future Massachusetts Governor and U.S. Senator.

cross_connections.display_label: Edwards's daughter refused his pulpit and her chair sat in the vestibule.

burialCemetery.name: Pine Hill Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Westfield Hampden County MA. Burial site of William Hooker Jr. (H01410) and Octavia Hale Hooker (I01124). FindAGrave Cemetery #91456.
